parentQuestion: To test CN V (trigeminal) motor function, you would ask the patient to ___.

scaffolds.q: CN V (trigeminal) controls the muscles used for chewing.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The masseter and other chewing muscles are controlled by CN V.

scaffolds.q: Motor function means the ability to move muscles.
Doctors test nerves by asking patients to do specific movements.
scaffolds.explain: Right! Each nerve controls specific muscles.

scaffolds.q: Clenching your jaw requires the muscles that close your mouth.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! These chewing muscles are controlled by CN V.

scaffolds.q: Smiling uses facial muscles controlled by CN VII, not CN V.
Sticking out your tongue uses CN XII (hypoglossal), not CN V.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Tongue movement is CN XII.

scaffolds.q: If CN V motor function is damaged, the patient would have trouble chewing.
scaffolds.explain: Exactly! Jaw muscles cannot contract properly.

scaffolds.q: The masseter muscle is used for biting and chewing.
scaffolds.explain: The masseter is innervated by CN V. Feel it on your cheek when you clench.

scaffolds.q: Testing jaw clench strength tests the motor component of CN V.
scaffolds.explain: Weakness in jaw clenching indicates trigeminal motor nerve damage.
